era_context: The High Middle Ages; the investiture controversy between the papacy and the Holy Roman Empire continues. In Iberia, the Reconquista is advancing. The Byzantine Empire is stabilizing under Alexios I Komnenos. In East Asia, the Song Dynasty rules China; Japan is in the late Heian period. The Islamic world is experiencing political fragmentation.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia beyond Islamic sources (limited documentation)

events.title: Norman conquest of Sicily completed; Treaty of Melfi aftermath
events.description: The Norman conquest of Sicily, which began in 1060, is largely completed by 1091 with the fall of Noto, the last Arab stronghold on the island. The conquest, led by Robert Guiscard and his brother Roger I, represents the end of Arab rule in Sicily and the establishment of Norman dominance in the Mediterranean. The conquest has profound consequences for the region: it brings Sicily into the Latin Christian world, establishes Norman feudal structures, and creates a new power base for Norman expansion. The conquest also marks the beginning of the Norman Kingdom of Sicily, which will become one of the most important medieval states.
key_figures: Roger I (Count of Sicily, Norman conqueror)
Robert Guiscard (Duke of Apulia, Norman leader)

sources.name: Gesta Normannorum Italicorum by William of Apulia (near-contemporary chronicle)

events.certainty_note: The completion of the Norman conquest of Sicily by 1091 is well-attested in multiple near-contemporary sources. The fall of Noto is dated to 1091 in most chronicles.

events.title: Cnut IV of Denmark dies; succession crisis
events.description: King Cnut IV (Canute the Holy) of Denmark dies on July 10, 1086 (or possibly 1091 in some sources, though 1086 is more reliable). [Note: The dating of Cnut IV's death is uncertain; most sources place it in 1086, not 1091. This entry is included with low confidence.] His death marks a transition in Danish politics and the succession of his brother Olaf I. Cnut IV had been known for his efforts to strengthen the Danish church and his military campaigns.
key_figures: Cnut IV (King of Denmark, possibly deceased)

sources.name: Heimskringla by Snorri Sturluson (13th-century Norse chronicle, later compilation)

events.certainty_note: The dating of Cnut IV's death is uncertain; most sources place it in 1086, not 1091. This entry is included with low confidence and should be verified against more reliable Scandinavian sources.
cross_references: 1086-001 (Possible correct date for Cnut IV's death)

events.title: Anund Jacob of Sweden possibly dies (uncertain date)
events.description: King Anund Jacob of Sweden may have died in 1091, though the exact date is uncertain and sources vary between 1093 and 1095. Anund Jacob had ruled for approximately 50 years and was known for his efforts to Christianize Sweden and strengthen royal authority. His death marks a transition in the Stenkil dynasty and Swedish politics.
key_figures: Anund Jacob (King of Sweden, possibly deceased)

sources.name: Heimskringla by Snorri Sturluson (13th-century Norse chronicle, later compilation)

events.certainty_note: The date of Anund Jacob's death is highly uncertain; sources vary between 1091, 1093, and 1095. The Heimskringla is a later compilation and not a contemporary source. Swedish records from this period are sparse.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Cnut IV's death is disputed: most sources place it in 1086, not 1091. The exact year depends on source reliability and calendar conversion. Anund Jacob's death date is highly uncertain; sources vary between 1091, 1093, and 1095. The significance of the Norman conquest of Sicily has sometimes been overstated as a direct precursor to the Crusades, though it did establish Norman military power in the Mediterranean.
historiographic_note: The year 1091 is moderately well-documented in Western European sources (particularly for the Norman conquest of Sicily) but less well-attested in Scandinavian sources. The historical record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Scandinavian sources for this period are sparse and often later compilations. The biases of literate elites (clergy, nobility, court historians) shape the surviving record.

graph_edges.note: The completion of the Norman conquest of Sicily led to the establishment of the Norman Kingdom of Sicily
Norman military success in Sicily was contemporary with the First Crusade
